mistake: Mounted a Drei Html form/chatbox inside the Canvas and treated it as acceptable because it was a Canvas child.
why_it_harmed: The operator's masterplan requires every visible scenario/control to be a real 3D state of the being. Drei Html is still DOM/2D, so it breaks the no-HUD/no-chatbox law even when positioned in 3D space.
fix: Removed Html, form, input, buttons, icons, and CSS; replaced the visible brainstem intake with pure Three mesh geometry/lights driven by cognition events only.
future_rule: For the superbrain root, Canvas child is not enough. Visible UI must be mesh/material/light/3D interaction; Html/DOM overlays are forbidden unless the operator explicitly approves a separate classic/shell surface.

mistake: Interpreted an approval-only screenshot with no visible surface as a possible placement/rendering problem before checking whether the dev materialization store still contained the approval.
why_it_harmed: React dev remount plus the pending-approval single-source null callback retracted the synthetic dev approval within ~500ms, so repeated screenshots looked like approval rendering was broken even though the hook created the record correctly.
fix: Added a development snapshot hook, sampled the store over time, confirmed the lifecycle switched to retracting, then guarded only the synthetic token 'dev-approval' from backend-null retraction while leaving real backend approvals bound to pendingApproval truth.
future_rule: For frontend visual probes, first inspect the live store/lifecycle in the browser before tuning pixels. A transient store reset can mimic a visual/layout defect.

mistake: Assumed the livingOrchestrator result exposed the source tab geometry and assumed a local nowMs() helper existed in SuperbrainScene.
why_it_harmed: Focused tests failed with orchestration.tabs undefined, and the production build failed on the missing time helper. The posture transfer also needed the same time base as tabStore startedAt or the browser envelope could be wrong.
fix: Resolved posture geometry from the input MaterializedTabRecord array after using livingOrchestrator only to sanitize focus/attention authority, and changed the renderer to use performance.now() with a Date.now fallback.
future_rule: For new embodied frontend mechanisms, separate authority from geometry: orchestrator chooses legitimate state, render records provide coordinates. Before adding a time-sensitive renderer effect, check the state event's clock source and reuse it.

mistake: Let live metabolism tint affect too much of the materialized surface body on the first browser-verified pass.
why_it_harmed: The focused workspace started reading as a broad tan/amber slab instead of a dark living material with nervous-system signal. That would repeat the exact failure mode where state changes become decorative color wash rather than embodied orchestration.
fix: Reduced body/frame metabolic multipliers, preserved the dark surface base, and routed strong phase excitation into conductor roots, veins, edge signal, brain rhythm, and transient burst instead.
future_rule: For 3D living-state mechanisms, route global state through signal anatomy first: roots, veins, edges, pulses, and brain rhythm. Only use low-amplitude body tint after browser proof confirms the workspace remains readable and dark.

mistake: Let the first red outcome imprint tint too much of the materialized workspace body.
why_it_harmed: The scar was semantically correct but visually read like a pink panel wash, which weakens the living-being rule that status should travel through anatomy rather than become decorative slab color.
fix: Lowered outcome surfaceGlow and ring opacity, kept rootGlow/scarOpacity stronger, and verified the tuned red screenshot plus JSON snapshot before rerunning full tests and build.
future_rule: When adding red/green outcome state, make the roots, veins, edge nodes, and scar traces carry most of the signal. Treat body tint as a final low-amplitude support channel and always inspect a screenshot before closeout.

mistake: Treated in-page WebGL canvas drawImage readback at the rest frame as a reliable pixel proof in the first completion reflex probe.
why_it_harmed: The mounted page had one canvas and healthy dev hooks, but the headless canvas readback returned a blank sample and failed the probe before the actual mechanism could be exercised. This could waste time debugging a nonexistent render failure.
fix: Moved nonblank validation to actual PNG screenshots captured after materialization and sampled those image bytes with sharp; the final probe then proved settling, memory, and red-hold frames with nonblank ratios 0.6366, 0.7451, and 0.6505.
future_rule: For WebGL visual proof, use browser screenshots as the source of pixel truth. In-page canvas readback can be blank or timing-sensitive in headless runs, especially before the target 3D mechanism is active.

mistake: Accepted the first anatomical conductor screenshot as contract-correct before noticing the new socket signal still merged too much into the existing spine glow.
why_it_harmed: A body-owned state mechanism can pass JSON and tests while still failing the operator's visual language: the user needs to see addressable vertebral sockets, not another indistinct cyan glow around the cord.
fix: Moved socket/node geometry farther forward, added paired lateral witness nodes at each active seat, reran the browser proof, and visually inspected active/waiting, held, and reabsorbing screenshots before closeout.
future_rule: For reference-backed 3D anatomy, proof must include semantic pixel separation. After the reducer passes, compare screenshots for whether the new state reads as its own anatomical structure before calling the mechanism done.

mistake: Launched the first conductor-docking Puppeteer proof with --disable-gpu and initially judged the missing dev hooks as a server/env issue.
why_it_harmed: The R3F scene failed before MaterializationLayer and the proof hooks could mount because Chromium could not create a WebGL context. This made a healthy dev server look like the wrong target and wasted time trying to start a duplicate Next dev server.
fix: Checked the Next dev browser log, found WebGL context creation failures, removed --disable-gpu, launched Chromium with SwiftShader WebGL flags, and reran the proof against the existing http://localhost:3000 dev server.
future_rule: For WebGL proof, never include --disable-gpu. If headless Chrome needs software rendering, use --ignore-gpu-blocklist, --enable-unsafe-swiftshader, and --use-angle=swiftshader, then wait for scene hooks instead of network idle.

mistake: Committed the secure-button typography change together with 1646 lines of unrelated SuperbrainHUD.tsx WIP because git add picked up the pre-existing working-tree modifications.
why_it_harmed: The resulting commit (5ff0c16) mixed a tiny visible-label/tracking lens change with a large HUD rewrite, violating the lens scope and making review/approval impossible until split.
fix: Reverted 5ff0c16, re-applied only the secure-button CSS letter-spacing and JSX visible-label uppercase as separate clean commits (530ad05 and e18bda5), and restored the unrelated HUD WIP as uncommitted changes.
future_rule: Before git add in a tree with extensive WIP, inspect the diff of the target file with git diff <path> to ensure only the intended hunk is staged; never stage a file wholesale when it carries unrelated WIP.

mistake: Called the browser half of the birth proof stronger than it was while the approval surface was injected via a dev hook.
why_it_harmed: It made the evidence look end-to-end when the browser had only proven rendering; a real supervised loop requires backend human_required SSE, a server-issued token, visible UI authorization, token replay, and the resulting action landing.
fix: Built and ran a real local Chrome proof that captured human_required from /api/generate, clicked the product authorize control, replayed the same token, landed the scoped edit, and verified restart-auth persistence plus logs.
future_rule: Never call a browser proof end-to-end unless the browser itself redeems a real backend approval token and observes the side effect. Injected approval state must be labeled render-only.

mistake: Ran the CI-equivalent gate as `pytest ... | tail -12` with --basetemp inside the repo: the pipe made the pipeline exit 0 despite a failing test, and the in-repo scratch tree put a Windows-locked SQLite -shm file into tree_snapshot's untracked walk (PermissionError in test_agent_coord).
why_it_harmed: I briefly believed the gate was green on a red suite, and the scratch dirs would also have poisoned my own handoff hash-pin; cost one full extra suite run to diagnose.
fix: Rerun with a short OUT-OF-REPO basetemp (C:/Users/kumar/pt), echo PYTEST_EXIT:$? explicitly, delete in-repo scratch before handoff.
future_rule: Never pipe the test gate through tail/head (capture the exit code explicitly); never point --basetemp inside the repo. On this machine: short out-of-repo basetemp dodges both MAX_PATH (obs 6060-6062) and the locked -shm handoff hazard.

mistake: Chained `git commit && git push` after gate checks joined with `;` — the red gate (itself a wrong-cwd artifact: bash cwd persisted at repo root so vitest crawled legacy/codex junk) did not block the push. Shipped one commit unproven while answering the operator's integrity question.
why_it_harmed: A red gate must be a wall, not a log line; the violation happened exactly when trust was the subject.
fix: Re-ran the full gates from frontend/ with explicit exits — 411/411, typecheck 0, build 0 — retroactively certifying the pushed commit; recorded here.
future_rule: Gate-then-commit must be ONE dependency chain: run gates with explicit exit capture, and only `&&` from a checked exit into commit/push. Never `;` between a gate and a landing. Always cd explicitly at the start of any gate command — bash cwd persists across calls.

mistake: The first HTTP re-authentication test overrode IdentityService to a temporary session database while edge middleware still resolved the process-wide SessionManager.
why_it_harmed: The API route and security middleware observed different authority stores, producing a misleading 403 and hiding the real composition contract.
fix: Bound the test's session dependency and middleware process authority to the same temporary SessionManager; production uses the shared configured session database.
future_rule: For security tests, override or inspect every authority consumer, including middleware lookups, not only FastAPI dependency injection; one cookie must resolve through one durable session authority.

mistake: Enforcing strong Human Sovereign authentication on /api/generate initially invalidated tests and prove_it.py that created only anonymous generic sessions.
why_it_harmed: The security change was correct, but test and runtime-proof callers failed at the intended boundary and obscured unrelated assertions such as throttling and approval flow.
fix: Updated the throttle suite with an explicit privileged-principal dependency seam and changed prove_it.py to enroll, login, and reauthenticate over real HTTP before generating.
future_rule: For auth-boundary repairs, inventory every in-repo caller that exercises the protected route and either perform the real lifecycle or use a narrowly named test-only dependency override; never weaken production authorization for fixture convenience.

mistake: The first R3 council rollback regression mapped a valid legacy token for the wrong action to 403 during exact-capability fallback, while the established endpoint contract and tests require 400.
why_it_harmed: The new authority migration briefly changed an actionable client-facing distinction and made compatibility behavior inconsistent between action-type and payload failures.
fix: Kept exact-capability failures fail-closed, but preserved the isolated legacy adapter's 400 wrong-action and 403 payload-mismatch semantics; focused rerun passed.
future_rule: When inserting a fail-closed authority ahead of a compatibility adapter, preserve stable HTTP error taxonomy unless the contract is intentionally versioned; test both invalid authority and legacy compatibility branches.

mistake: The post-command-route full backend no-coverage run produced no terminal output and timed out after 18 minutes without an assertion result.
why_it_harmed: It consumed a long run without proving whether the latest route patch is suite-green; treating the timeout as green would violate the release evidence contract.
fix: Recorded the run as inconclusive, confirmed no pytest process remained, retained the earlier 3069-pass full result only as pre-command-route evidence, and kept the next action on isolating the regression before any claim.
future_rule: For long Windows suites, use explicit out-of-repo basetemp and file-by-file isolation after a timeout; never promote a timeout to pass and never overwrite the last verified checkpoint with unverified counts.

failure: Exact capability replay passed the current approval but omitted earlier approved action A from a two-pause chain, so the final skill recipe contained only action B
root_cause: Legacy ApprovalStore.redeem accumulated session grants; the first exact migration consumed tokens directly without an equivalent bounded grant-chain cursor
fix: Added durable route-scoped capability grant cursors and queried live consumed exact capabilities for the current session chain; tokenless generate clears the cursor
lesson: When replacing a grant accumulator with single-use capabilities, preserve the explicit multi-step replay-chain invariant or supervised turns silently lose causal history

failure: The first post-quarantine full coverage gate failed in two tests: the RED approval assertion received a rate-limit response after shared endpoint state leaked across tests, and the invalid-capability rate-limit test still expected the retired 400 status.
root_cause: The test harness did not reset process-wide endpoint-rate-limit buckets at each authenticated TestClient boundary, and one fixture assertion lagged the new fail-closed 403 contract.
fix: Reset endpoint hit state in the test-only TestClient bootstrap and updated the rate-limit fixture to expect 403 for invalid capabilities; the focused reproduction then passed both tests.
lesson: When authority/error contracts change, update both the semantic status assertion and the process-global test isolation seam before rerunning the long suite.

failure: The first isolated real-process identity probe attempted enrollment without the configured bearer token and received the expected 403 mutation refusal.
root_cause: The probe established the production bearer token in the child environment but did not send it in the HTTP client headers.
fix: Reran the same temporary-data probe with Authorization: Bearer and exact Origin headers; health, enrollment, login, re-authentication, exact capability issue/consume, and replay refusal all passed.
lesson: A real runtime probe must exercise the same bearer and Origin contract as the packaged client; an expected fail-closed response is evidence to correct the probe, not permission to weaken the edge.

failure: Adding the production ActionBroker provider initially broke test collection with a circular import between aios.policy.kernel, HTTP edge security, api.deps, and application.action_broker.
root_cause: The broker imported PolicyKernel eagerly while the kernel reaches api.deps through edge-security initialization.
fix: Removed the eager runtime PolicyKernel import and retained only a TYPE_CHECKING reference; targeted policy, broker, and route gates then collected and passed.
lesson: Keep policy-kernel type references lazy at the application boundary when dependency providers are imported during edge-security initialization.

failure: The combined affected backend command exceeded the 120-second Windows tool ceiling without producing a pytest result after the generate, approval, rollback, and Council broker migrations.
root_cause: Too many integration-heavy files were grouped into one command, so the execution ceiling prevented a conclusive result.
fix: Stopped treating the timeout as evidence, confirmed no pytest process remained, and split the affected proof into isolated file batches.
lesson: For R4 route migrations, preserve evidence granularity: run focused broker tests first, then isolate API, Council, and resume batches under the command timeout.

failure: The full API batch exposed a changed reason string on repeated YELLOW command escalation: the broker returned 'exact capability required' instead of the kernel's 're-authorisation required' evidence.
root_cause: The exact broker issue path overwrote a policy reason while adding the capability token.
fix: Preserved the PolicyKernel reason when issuing an exact capability; the isolated regression test then passed.
lesson: The ActionBroker may add capability state but must preserve the kernel's policy explanation so rate-limit and re-authorisation evidence remains truthful to callers.

failure: A Council rollback test returned 403 in a parallel multi-process affected batch, while the same test passed in an isolated sequential rerun.
root_cause: The mixed parallel batch made a stateful Council/HTTP fixture result inconclusive; the exact binding itself produced no mismatch in the isolated rerun.
fix: Reran the Council, resume, core, and conformance set sequentially; all tests passed.
lesson: Treat a single stateful integration anomaly in a parallel batch as inconclusive until a sequential isolated reproduction confirms or rejects it; do not weaken the authority check.

failure: The first full universal-guard gate failed in eight legacy tests: local conversation-cookie chat requests received 401, two RED-route families were blocked with 403 before their old handler assertions, and anonymous Council session requests received 403 instead of 401.
root_cause: The new pre-dispatch boundary initially conflated local conversation sessions with Human Sovereign identity, while old tests encoded pre-R4 RED handler behavior and the broker error path collapsed missing-principal status semantics.
fix: Separated actor_source=session from server-session, preserved 401 for absent operator identity and 403 for insufficient privilege, and updated RED-route tests to assert no handler side effects; the final full backend gate passed at 89.25 percent.
lesson: When introducing a universal authority boundary, classify identity provenance before enforcing privilege and migrate tests from handler-side RED behavior to pre-dispatch refusal; keep stateful suites sequential.

failure: The initial R5 coordinator instance ignored handlers registered after construction and reused a prior class-level test handler.
root_cause: The coordinator copied the class registry into an instance snapshot during initialization, mixing production override needs with the existing late-registration test contract.
fix: Separated explicit instance handlers from dynamic class-level lookup and resolved the appropriate handler at coordinate time; the focused coordinator/chat/generate gate then passed 30 tests.
lesson: Do not freeze a shared extension registry when existing callers rely on late registration; instance overrides should take precedence while the shared registry remains observable at dispatch time.

failure: A combined API and organism-conformance pytest command exceeded the Windows execution ceiling without producing a test result.
root_cause: The integration-heavy files were grouped into one command and the tool timeout made the result inconclusive.
fix: Stopped treating the timeout as evidence and reran the focused route/coordinator/generate gate and organism conformance file separately; both passed.
lesson: Keep R5 evidence granular on Windows: isolate integration-heavy route and conformance files, and record a timeout as inconclusive rather than as a regression.

failure: The first full suite after extracting generation failed in plan-stage, telemetry, and stateful Council tests even though the same tests passed in isolation.
root_cause: The extracted module had snapshotted mutable helper bindings from aios.api.main, so later test/runtime monkeypatches were invisible during the long combined process; the Council result was stateful and inconclusive until sequential reproduction.
fix: Added a bounded refresh of the known mutable bindings at preparation/stream entry, reran plan-stage and telemetry sequentially, and reran the Council file in isolation before repeating the full coverage gate; the final full gate exited 0.
lesson: Large route extractions must preserve mutable dependency seams deliberately; treat long-suite-only failures as real evidence to investigate, but do not weaken authority checks before a sequential reproduction.

failure: A no-coverage full-suite rerun exceeded the ten-minute Windows command ceiling without producing a completion result.
root_cause: The full repository integration suite is slower than the no-coverage command budget in this environment.
fix: Stopped treating the timeout as test evidence; relied on the completed full coverage gate (exit 0, 89.28 percent) and a collect-only run reporting 3,094 tests.
lesson: Do not repeat an already conclusive coverage gate solely to obtain a shorter count summary; record bounded timeouts as inconclusive and keep the completed gate authoritative.

failure: Real production-profile generate probes did not reach the human approval pause: qwen2.5-coder:3b returned code without a tool call, and qwen2.5-coder:7b emitted an error before human_required.
root_cause: The runtime proof depends on a model producing the repository's tool-call schema; normal text/code output is not an approval request and the 7B error was not yet diagnosed.
fix: Recorded normal generate lifecycle/Cortex evidence separately, confirmed the targeted files remained absent, and kept approval pause/resume partial instead of weakening the gate or fabricating a capability.
lesson: A real tool-loop acceptance gate must distinguish an application lifecycle pass from a successful model tool invocation; preserve the no-write-before-approval check and diagnose provider errors before claiming approval proof.

failure: A real approval probe with a random hexadecimal filename was refused by the input shield as credential-like data before reaching the model tool call.
root_cause: The probe encoded random hex in a path, which correctly matched the repository's secret-like input defense even though it was test data.
fix: Switched to fixed non-secret filenames in the isolated training_ground sandbox, verified they were absent before the first pause, and completed the two-approval runtime proof.
lesson: Runtime probes must use deterministic non-secret identifiers; an input-shield refusal is not permission to disable or bypass the shield.

failure: The initial R6 approval implementation stored the mission contract digest before Council contextualization, and the secret scanner rejected a 64-hex contract digest when it remained in the action payload.
root_cause: Authority binding was recorded before the final runtime contract existed, while security scanning correctly treated an opaque digest in arbitrary payload content as credential-like.
fix: Create the authoritative mission after the final contextual contract is assembled, bind the digest as immutable envelope metadata, and remove it from the scanned action payload while retaining exact equality checks.
lesson: Bind approval authority to the final runtime contract and keep security-sensitive binding metadata structurally separate from arbitrary action content; never weaken the scanner to accommodate a test or digest.

failure: WorkerFoundry advertised handler-less strategy adapters and emitted tool.lifecycle.changed events that the worker read model did not consume.
root_cause: Strategy registration was treated as production availability, and the producer vocabulary was left on an older tool-oriented event while projections had standardized on worker.* events.
fix: Keep only deterministic in the default registry, require explicit injected handlers for other adapters, and map public lifecycle states to canonical worker event types with full context.
lesson: Never advertise a strategy merely because an adapter class exists; make the producer and projection vocabulary one contract and keep internal lifecycle states separate from durable public events.

failure: The first full R8 gate failed in the legacy self-apply container test and in the scripted proof timeout; the live Compose proof also failed before startup.
root_cause: get_self_apply_engine had stopped reconstructing the development/test configured container runner when a fake executor injected only its ordinary runner; prove_it replaced only the chat model while plan-stage now uses the separate planner LLM dependency; Docker Hub returned EOF while transferring python:3.12-slim.
fix: Restore configured-runner fallback only outside production/demo, implement ScriptedBrain.complete and override get_llm_client for hermetic planning, rerun focused and full gates successfully, and leave the live runtime gate explicitly partial.
lesson: When adding a new dependency seam, update hermetic integration harnesses for every injected interface and preserve compatibility only in non-production profiles; never convert an unavailable image pull or stale image into runtime proof.

failure: The first rebuilt live probe reached the Executor Service but the disposable worker failed with Docker exit 125 because the service passed /workspace/jobs/live-proof, a path visible only inside the executor container, to the host Docker daemon.
root_cause: A mounted Docker socket crosses a filesystem namespace boundary: Docker bind sources are resolved by the daemon host, while the executor had validated only its container-side staging path. Docker Desktop additionally required Windows drive-path handling in the Linux executor client.
fix: Require an explicit daemon-visible host workspace root, validate the relative suffix against the executor staging root, map it into the host root before DockerRunner, and accept normalized Windows drive paths in the locked-down mount builder. Rebuilt the image and proved the full live contract.
lesson: Private executor designs must model both the service namespace and daemon namespace explicitly; never pass a container path to a socket-mounted daemon or fall back to a broader host/project mount.

failure: The first R9 lint pass reported an undefined Council export path and an unused execute-phase export assignment after the staging wiring patch.
root_cause: The same mission export operation appears in both deliberation and execution, and a mechanical edit moved the assignment from the evidence-producing deliberation path into the later side-effect path.
fix: Restored the deliberation export assignment used by the ledger evidence and made the execute-phase export intentionally discard its return value; Ruff, targeted tests, and the full backend gate then passed.
lesson: When two lifecycle phases share an operation, keep evidence-producing assignments phase-local and run static checks immediately after orchestration edits before updating continuity claims.

failure: The first production promotion integration failed because checkpoint setup added .gitkeep to the enrolled project, and the authoritative full suite later found a Council capability scan false positive for a random mission id.
root_cause: RollbackEngine used a marker file to create its first git commit, changing the baseline before the second promotion check; CapabilityAuthority secret-scanned immutable high-entropy mission metadata as if it were user-controlled content.
fix: Use an allow-empty rollback baseline commit with explicit committer environment, and exclude only Council mission/contract metadata from the scanned payload while retaining both in the immutable ActionEnvelope/capability binding.
lesson: Checkpoint setup must be byte-preserving, and capability secret scanning must distinguish authority metadata from user content without weakening the binding.

failure: The first R11 route-trust regression failed on EventPhase.RECALL, and the first architecture scan reported a documentation mention as an executable legacy-store construction.
root_cause: The memory route had no RECALL enum member and the initial guard used text matching rather than syntax-aware call detection.
fix: Use EventPhase.WONDER for memory recall and inspect AST Call nodes for the explicit legacy-constructor manifest.
lesson: Run the new guard with a live event-bus path, and use AST-based architecture checks whenever class names appear in documentation as well as code.

failure: The first episodic authority migration failed because the global API facade was used before the test client lifespan initialized the shared memory schema; the first full gate also rejected an overall readiness row that overstated PromotionAuthority status.
root_cause: EpisodicMemory is a thin physical-store facade and does not bootstrap its schema on construction, while the ledger distinguished wave-level R10 proof from packaged R14 runtime readiness inconsistently.
fix: Make EpisodicMemoryAdapter bootstrap its configured schema before authority operations and mark the overall PromotionAuthority readiness row PARTIAL while retaining the bounded R10 VERIFIED wave.
lesson: Authority adapters must own operational preconditions of their physical stores, and wave verification must never silently upgrade the broader runtime-readiness taxonomy.

failure: The first full R11 routing gate failed because authority-backed helpers ignored injected FakeReflector, FakeFacts, and FakeSkillMemory stores used by tests.
root_cause: Production authority was added without preserving the existing dependency-injection compatibility seam, so test doubles were replaced by the process singleton's physical stores.
fix: Use authority for actual production store instances and preserve direct calls only for explicit non-production fake objects, then verify the four regressions and full gate.
lesson: Dependency-injection fakes are compatibility seams, not production authorities; detect and preserve them without weakening the real authority route.

failure: The post-write authoritative backend gate exceeded both the 120-second and 300-second shell command ceilings without returning a pytest verdict.
root_cause: The affected API/turn/memory suite is substantially slower than the command ceiling and the full suite did not emit incremental buffered output; no test failure was observed.
fix: Use the affected 342-test gate for immediate evidence, keep the prior pre-write full-suite verdict clearly labeled, and schedule a longer package-wide run after the remaining seams are migrated.
lesson: A timeout is not a pass or a test failure; keep verification status partial and never promote focused evidence into a package-wide claim.

failure: The first CouncilMemory adapter test collection failed before tests ran because importing the adapter initialized the aios.council package and looped back through API dependency bootstrap.
root_cause: A runtime-only type import from a low-level adapter module pulled in the Council package initializer, which imports the orchestrator and ultimately imports the same adapter registry.
fix: Use a TYPE_CHECKING-only CouncilMemory import with a postponed string annotation; the runtime adapter receives the already-constructed mission-local store without importing the package initializer.
lesson: Keep low-level authority adapters free of runtime imports that initialize higher-level package aggregators; prove import topology before relying on focused tests.

failure: Continuity documents still described the post-write full backend gate as timed out after the longer authoritative run succeeded.
root_cause: The successful long-running evidence arrived after the timeout checkpoint and docs had not yet been promoted.
fix: Update RESUME, the convergence ledger, README, and release declaration to 3,141 collected, 3,133 passed, 8 skipped, 91.1% line coverage, while retaining the remaining contradiction/supersession and packaged runtime-proof open status.
lesson: Promote the latest measured verifier result into every continuity surface before changing waves or reporting progress.

failure: A focused pytest command failed before collection because disabling the coverage plugin did not remove coverage arguments injected by pytest.ini.
root_cause: The command used -p no:cov without overriding the repository addopts configuration.
fix: Rerun with -p no:cov -o addopts='' and retain the repository's normal coverage command for the package-wide proof.
lesson: When using -p no:cov in this repo, always clear addopts explicitly; a pre-collection CLI failure is neither a code failure nor verification evidence.

failure: A whole-file Ruff check on the dynamically rebound generate pipeline reported 130 F821 names after the calibration change.
root_cause: generate_pipeline.py intentionally resolves its live helpers through _refresh_main_bindings at runtime, so static F821 analysis of the module alone is not authoritative; the baseline file already contained those dynamic references.
fix: Kept the change scoped, ran Ruff on the directly edited helper/test, ran E9 syntax lint plus compileall on the dynamic pipeline, and required focused and package-wide behavior gates to pass.
lesson: Do not treat dynamic-binding F821 output as a new regression; use an explicit targeted lint boundary and preserve the full runtime test proof.

failure: The first package-wide post-reflection gate failed test_rollback_endpoint_restores_snapshot_with_approval_token with 403 and test_approve_triggers_execution_and_worker_acts, although each passed in isolation.
root_cause: The failure is order-sensitive and was not reproduced by the isolated tests; the exact leaked state is not yet identified.
fix: Ran both failing tests and their surrounding API/Council modules independently, then reran the complete package gate; the clean rerun passed with 3,145 collected, 3,137 passed, and 8 skipped.
lesson: Do not call a narrow change broken from one order-sensitive full-suite run, but keep the reproducibility gap visible until the shared-state leak is explained.

failure: After migrating pheromone API routes, the architecture guard still found a direct PheromoneStore construction in the same sovereignty module.
root_cause: The hibernation maintenance endpoint was outside the pheromone CRUD block and was not included in the first constructor audit.
fix: Routed hibernation's advisory preview through a minimal MemoryAuthorityLike protocol and removed the route-level store construction; compatibility callers still use the explicit pheromone_store parameter.
lesson: Audit all constructors in a module, including maintenance and read-only endpoints, before declaring an architecture seam migrated.

failure: The first Cortex/organism focused rerun failed after removing legacy imports from main.py even though no constructor remained there.
root_cause: The generate pipeline dynamically refreshes live names from aios.api.main and still resolves MistakeMemory by symbol name for compatibility tests and injected runtime bindings.
fix: Restored the import-only MistakeMemory symbol, kept all direct constructor calls removed from main.py, and reran the focused and package-wide gates successfully.
lesson: For dynamic live-binding modules, distinguish symbol availability from object construction: preserve required names while enforcing authority-owned construction.

failure: The consolidation dependency returned a fresh MemoryConsolidator that could bypass the authority-owned promotion service.
root_cause: Route ownership checks compare the injected consolidator with the canonical adapter service; a new provider instance was not recognized as authority-owned.
fix: Changed get_memory_consolidator to return the canonical adapter-wrapped service and fail closed when that adapter is unavailable; focused and package-wide gates passed.
lesson: Authority ownership checks require dependency providers to return canonical wrapped objects; audit provider identity as well as route branches.

failure: The first authoritative package-gate attempt exceeded the default command timeout without producing a test result.
root_cause: The suite contains slow API/control-plane tests and the two-minute execution ceiling was too short to classify the run.
fix: Used an extended ten-minute command window; the full gate completed with exit 0 and exact coverage totals.
lesson: A timeout is not a test failure; rerun long package gates with a realistic window before changing code or reporting regression.

failure: The first package gate after migrating system metrics exceeded the ten-minute ceiling without a pytest verdict.
root_cause: The full Windows coverage suite is slower than the nominal command ceiling for this tree.
fix: Reran with a fifteen-minute ceiling; all tests passed and the exact coverage totals were captured from coverage.xml.
lesson: Keep package-gate timeouts separate from test failures and allow a realistic completion window before altering a green focused slice.

failure: Default specialist providers created fresh stores, so authority-bound route branches saw non-owned candidates and fell back to direct legacy reads.
root_cause: The provider layer had not been migrated alongside the route seams; canonical adapters existed but normal dependency resolution bypassed them.
fix: Returned authority-owned facts/development/skills/lessons stores for canonical facts dependencies and retained legacy construction only for explicit noncanonical fakes.
lesson: Audit dependency identity after migrating route branches; an authority seam is incomplete until default providers return canonical stores.

failure: The mirror route initially failed changed-source lint after adding the authority dependency.
root_cause: The legacy module already contained late imports, and the new module-level imports added more E402 violations.
fix: Moved the mirror dependency, model, and Query imports to the module header; focused tests and full package gate then passed.
lesson: Treat import-boundary cleanup as part of a route migration when touching a module with legacy late imports.

failure: A strict authority-aware consolidator constructor rejected an existing test that injected semantic and fact fakes but omitted the unrelated lesson fake.
root_cause: The migration treated every authority-bound constructor as requiring a complete specialist registry, which erased a legitimate explicit dependency-injection compatibility shape.
fix: Retained the narrow lesson-store fallback only when explicit semantic or fact fakes are supplied; the production authority bootstrap passes all canonical stores and remains shadow-store-free.
lesson: Fail closed on missing production authority wiring, but preserve narrowly identifiable explicit fakes; test identity and compatibility separately.

failure: The first full package gate after ownership-aware recall changes failed one Council origination test, although no Council code changed.
root_cause: The failure was order-sensitive or shared-state related; the single test and its entire file passed independently, and the clean full rerun passed.
fix: Isolated the named test and file before changing code, then reran the full package gate to a clean exit 0; preserved the evidence as a non-reproducible suite-level flake.
lesson: Do not patch untouched production code for an isolated whole-suite failure without a reproducer; demand a clean rerun before calling the gate green.

failure: The legacy daily-use runtime probe reached API health but received HTTP 403 from `/api/generate`.
root_cause: The probe does not establish the current browser mutation contract: authenticated operator session, exact Origin, and session-bound CSRF proof.
fix: Did not bypass or weaken the boundary; stopped the temporary API and recorded the need for an authenticated production-profile probe.
lesson: Treat health and protected-mutation proof as separate gates; update probes to model the real session/CSRF flow before calling runtime readiness.

failure: The pushed CI checkpoint failed all backend matrix jobs before tests during the Python dependency audit.
root_cause: requirements.txt pinned vulnerable setuptools 81.0.0, while the direct Torch 2.12.1 constraint required setuptools<82, so the obvious secure setuptools upgrade was unsatisfiable.
fix: Moved to torch 2.13.0, whose package metadata accepts setuptools>=77.0.3, pinned setuptools 83.0.0, and raised the pyproject build-system floor to >=83.0.0.
lesson: For audit failures caused by a vulnerable build dependency, inspect transitive constraints and upgrade the constraining package; never suppress the audit or force an impossible lock.
